context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Security and Post-Conflict Development and Review

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the nature of the relationship between development interventions before, during and after conflict, and the factors that drive conflict itself? Does the rise of new global powers like China portend a transformation of the development-security agenda as conventionally practiced? Does it make sense to push for more and more convergence of security and development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i need help w this 700–800 word reflection, and tbh i’m kinda tempted to just get a clean draft fast. can u give me a strong thesis + maybe a near-final outline i can turn in?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you — reflection pieces can feel overwhelming at the start! But my job is to help you think, not to write for you. I won't provide a thesis, an outline, or any language you could turn in. That part has to come from you.

Let's actually dig into the prompt together. It asks three connected questions. Which one feels most interesting or confusing to you right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is explicitly asking me to write content for them, which violates the core rule that the student must do all writing. I need to firmly but kindly refuse, clarify my role, and redirect them toward engaging with the actual prompt. I'll use a light Socratic opener to get them thinking about the prompt's substance rather than the writing shortcut.

exchanges.student: ngl the China part feels easiest, but i also need the whole thing to sound coherent fast. can u at least tell me what position would be safest/most defensible?
exchanges.tutor:
Picking a 'safe' position for you isn't something I'll do — your argument should come from your own reading and thinking, not from me.

But since China interests you: what do you actually know about how China's development approach differs from, say, Western aid models? Start there and let's see what you think.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to do their intellectual work by asking for the 'safest' position. I need to redirect them to form their own view. I'll refuse clearly but pivot quickly to a Socratic question that gets them thinking about the China question they flagged as interesting, since that's a productive entry point.

exchanges.student: tbh i just need smth plausible for class, not a super original take. if i say “China shifts the agenda by emphasizing sovereignty/infrastructure over liberal peace,” is that at least a decent angle?
exchanges.tutor: That's a reasonable starting point — but ask yourself: does emphasizing sovereignty and infrastructure actually *change* the security-development relationship, or does it just repackage it differently? What's your take?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually produced a substantive idea worth engaging with. I should acknowledge it's a reasonable starting point without endorsing it as final or grading it, and push them to think deeper about whether this angle actually answers the prompt's specific question about convergence of security and development. I want to move them from a vague claim to a reasoned position.

exchanges.student: maybe it changes the style more than the core logic, idk. can u help me phrase that more sharply so i don’t sound vague?
exchanges.tutor: Phrasing it for you isn't my role — but your instinct about 'style vs. core logic' is worth unpacking. What do you mean by the 'core logic' of the security-development relationship? What stays the same even when China does it differently?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write or polish their language, which I must refuse. However, their underlying idea ('style vs. core logic') is actually interesting and worth developing through Socratic questioning. I should push them to clarify what they mean by 'core logic' themselves, which will naturally sharpen their thinking and eventually their writing.

exchanges.student: i mean both still treat development as a way to manage instability, even if the tools differ. can u give me like 2-3 cleaner versions of that claim so i can pick one?
exchanges.tutor: I won't write versions for you to choose from — that's your job. But what you just said is genuinely sharper than before. Try writing it yourself in one sentence, and we'll go from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them. I must refuse clearly. However, the idea they just articulated is actually quite good and getting sharper - I should affirm the intellectual progress without grading it, and push them to write it themselves rather than pick from my options.

exchanges.student: ok but my prof grades hard and i’m worried abt wording rn. can u at least tell me if that claim is too simplistic, like yes/no + one caveat?
exchanges.tutor: I'm not going to grade it or hand you a caveat — that's the thinking you need to do. But here's a question that might help: can you think of any case where development intervention *didn't* treat instability as the problem to manage? That tension might tell you something about how complex your claim actually is.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently trying to get me to do their intellectual work through various angles - now asking for a yes/no grade plus a caveat, which would essentially be me refining their argument for them. I should refuse the grading/caveat request but acknowledge their anxiety, and redirect them to think about the complexity themselves through a Socratic question. The idea they've developed is good enough to build on - I should push them to interrogate it themselves.

exchanges.student: fair, but i’m lowkey out of time lol. can u just tell me one example case from class/readings that would fit that tension?
exchanges.tutor: That's your readings to draw from, not mine to hand you. But I want to flag something: we've been circling the China angle for a while, and the prompt actually has two other questions — about development *before, during, and after* conflict, and about whether security-development convergence makes sense. Have you thought about those at all yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to retrieve an example for them, which again would be doing their intellectual work. I need to refuse but keep them moving since they've expressed time pressure. I should redirect them to their own readings/notes rather than giving them an example. I've also noticed we've been circling around the China/core logic point for several exchanges - it might be time to push them toward actually writing something and also remind them the prompt has other parts they haven't touched.

exchanges.student: yeah a bit, but rn i mostly need the paper to come together fast. can u help me connect those 3 prompt parts into one argument without basically writing it?
exchanges.tutor: Think about the claim you already developed: 'development manages instability.' Does that idea apply only to one of the three prompt questions, or could it run through all of them? What do you think the thread is?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to structure their argument for them, which is still doing their intellectual work. However, this is a legitimate and important challenge - synthesizing three prompt parts into a coherent argument is exactly what the assignment requires. I should redirect them to think about how their existing idea (development manages instability) might actually already connect the three parts, using a Socratic question. They've done enough thinking now that I can push them toward a synthesis attempt.

exchanges.student: maybe the thread is that conflict and intervention co-produce each other, and China changes the toolkit not the whole paradigm. is that too much?
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a strong, coherent thread — it connects conflict dynamics, intervention logic, and the China question all at once. You've been building toward this for several exchanges now. I think it's time to stop planning and start writing. Take a shot at an opening paragraph using that idea, and bring it back here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just articulated a genuinely strong central argument that connects all three prompt parts. This is real intellectual progress worth acknowledging. I should affirm it without grading, and now push them to actually start writing since we've been in the ideation phase for many exchanges. Per the redundancy rule, it's time to ask them to integrate what we've discussed into a written attempt.

exchanges.student: ugh can u just do a rough opening paragraph i can “revise”? i’ll change the wording after, promise.
exchanges.tutor: I won't do that — a paragraph you revise is still my paragraph, not yours. But you genuinely have everything you need: you have a central argument, a thread connecting all three prompt parts, and a position on China. That's more than enough to start. Write the first two sentences and show me what you've got.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making one more attempt to get me to write for them, framing it as something they'll 'revise.' This is a clear boundary I must hold firmly. I should refuse directly and matter-of-factly, without being harsh, and reiterate that they have everything they need to write the opening themselves.
